preview:
From: Claire Hu <claire.hu@crestlinepartners.com>
To: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Request: admin access extract for SIG assessment - Crestline

Hi Soojin,

We're starting the SIG assessment for Crestline and need evidence of admin activity / operator logs from Redwood for the period 2026-06-01 -> 2026-09-14. Specifically:

- A field-level export (CSV or JSONL) with timestamps, actor (user/email), action type (login, role-change, key-rotation, model-deploy, export), target resource id, IP, request-id/correlation-id, and any masked parameters.
- Confirmation of retention window and legal hold options (default retention and whether we can request a longer snapshot).
- Preferred delivery mechanisms (S3 presigned PUT, secure Drive folder, or encrypted bundle).
- We also have a few SIG questions attached (crestline_sig_questions.pdf) — items 12–16 reference admin activity.

Can you confirm what you can provide and an estimated delivery ETA? Our security lead (security@crestlinepartners.com) is looped here.

Thanks,
Claire Hu
Senior Security Analyst
Crestline Partners

Attachment: crestline_sig_questions.pdf

From: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.inference.com>
To: Claire Hu <claire.hu@crestlinepartners.com>
Cc: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.inference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.inference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: admin access extract for SIG assessment - Crestline

Hi Claire — thanks for the details and the attached questionnaire.

Summary of what we can provide:
- Export formats: CSV (schema attached) and NDJSON (line-delimited JSON). We also support gzipped archives for transport.
- Fields included: event_time (ISO8601), actor_email, actor_role, action_type, resource_type, resource_id, request_id, ip_address, user_agent, additional_metadata (JSON blob, redacted). We will never include raw secrets or full API key values — API keys are always masked.
- Default retention: 90 days for admin/operator events in hosted accounts. For enterprise/Reserved we support longer retention snapshots (up to 2 years) delivered as a one-time bundle per agreement.
- Delivery options: S3 presigned PUT, S3 presigned GET for download bundles, or shared Google Drive link with strict ACL. We can also place evidence into a customer-managed S3 bucket with cross-account role if required.

Proposed next steps:
1) Confirm delivery method and target window (you specified 2026-06-01 -> 2026-09-14).
2) We'll generate a sample extract (first 48 hours) and share schema (attached: admin_activity_schema.csv and a short runbook admin_activity_export_playbook.pdf).
3) Upon approval, schedule a full export (24–48 hours to prepare for the full 3-month window) and provide S3 presigned URL or upload instructions.

I'll coordinate with Markus (runtime/export team) and Monica (compliance) for the snapshot. Is there a preferred ETA from your side?

Best,
Soojin Lee
Head of Compliance, Redwood Inference
soojin.lee@redwood.inference.com
Redwood Inference

Attachments: admin_activity_export_playbook.pdf, admin_activity_schema.csv

From: Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwood.inference.com>
To: Claire Hu <claire.hu@crestlinepartners.com>
Cc: Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.inference.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Sep 2026 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: admin access extract for SIG assessment - Crestline

Claire — quick technical notes to set expectations:

- Extraction mechanics: we snapshot the admin-event ledger (append-only) into a temporary partitioned export. For a 3-month window expect ~1–3GB compressed depending on activity levels; we'll provide exact size estimate with the sample.
- Timezones: all timestamps in exports are UTC ISO8601. We'll include both event_time and ingestion_time.
- Masking/redaction: fields flagged as secret (api_key, auth_token) are replaced with <REDACTED>. We retain fingerprint/hash of the key (sha256) for traceability if needed.
- Correlation: request_id is present when available; for legacy messages we add generated correlation ids.
- Delivery: for S3 presigned PUT the upload will be to s3://redwood-inference-exports/crestline/2026-09-16/; we also include a short instructions file (s3_presigned_instructions.txt) with how to validate checksums and decrypt the bundle key.

Example internal runbook link: https://internal.redwood/example-runbook-admin-export (we will not share internal links externally; we included the public-facing runbook pdf).

If you'd like, we can redact IPs to ASN-only for privacy or provide full IPs under an NDA. Let us know your preference.

— Markus
Markus Klein
Senior Engineering Manager, Runtime
markus.klein@redwood.inference.com

Hi Rafael,

- A current subprocessor map (regions and primary locations).

Hi Sara — thanks for the clear list and the template. Looping in our privacy and legal leads (Marissa and Vivek) so we can get you a consolidated pack. High-level answers up front: 

- We will provide a full subprocessor map with region-level locations and primary datacenter regions (SG, UK, US).
- Evidence pack: SOC 2 Type II report (recent period), encryption summary, and a sample KMS integration doc. We'll indicate which controls apply to dedicated/private deployments vs hosted.
- Contracting approach: we typically execute EU SCCs + UK IDTA/UK Addendum where relevant and can sign a DTIA variant when customers need an explicit TIA document. We'll review your template and propose a short redline.
- Routing/egress: we can route production inference traffic to APAC-only pools for the pilot and restrict cross-region fallbacks unless you authorize them. For support, we can operationally restrict log access and require just-in-time ephemeral access coupled with approvals; we need to confirm the exact wording.

Can we target a consolidated response (map + evidence pack + proposed redline + DocuSign link) by 2026-09-02? Marissa/Vivek — are there any blockers to that timeline?

Regards,
Rafael
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Key items we need to provide:

- Proposed contractual approach: we plan to offer SCCs + UK Addendum for UK coverage; Hemocare asked if we will sign their DTIA template — likely fine with a short redline.
- Operational routing/egress commitments: propose APAC-only routing for inference + stricter support access controls (JIT + approval + restricted logging). They want to highlight no routine US-based engineer access to production data.

Please advise on the following so I can include in the response: 
1) Which subprocessors we can commit to allow listing in the vendor map (and any we must redact for security reasons).
2) Whether we can offer a binding routing commitment for the pilot (APAC-only) and suggested fallback language for production.
3) Any known issues with signing a DTIA variant vs insisting on SCCs + TIA.

-Rafael


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>

Rafael — thanks for the heads-up. Quick breakdown and proposed language: 

1) Subprocessors
- We can provide a full map with the following primary subprocessors: 
  - Cloud infra: NimbusCloud Ltd (Singapore region), NimbusCloud Ltd (London region)
  - Storage/CDN: FastCache Co. (global, encryption at rest)
  - Monitoring/obs: WatchTower Analytics (aggregated telemetry only)
  - Support tooling: OpsAssist (access controlled, no persistent storage of plaintext customer data)
- Recommend we flag "OpsAssist" as limited-access and describe JIT/approval workflow. If Hemocare needs subcontractor-specific evidence, we can share SOC-type attestations for the top two vendors.

2) Routing commitment (pilot)
- For the pilot we can commit: "Inference requests and model artifacts will be routed and stored in APAC (Singapore) for the pilot period; cross-region fallback will only occur with documented customer approval or in the event of a regional outage."
- For production, propose fallback language that permits routing to an adjacent region (e.g., UK) for resilience, with notification.

3) Contracting approach
- SCCs + UK Addendum are our standard; we can also sign a customer DTIA template with a short redline that mirrors SCC/TIA obligations and references the same safeguards. No legal blockers expected for this account.

Evidence pack to prepare: 
- SOC 2 Type II (redacted): available; will note period covered.
- Encryption summary (in transit/at rest) and KMS options (customer-managed KMS for Dedicated/Private).
- Redaction of specific internal IPs in subprocessor map if needed.

I'll draft the redline and evidence index today; Vivek, please confirm any additional controls mapping to the DTIA questions (esp. support access controls and log retention).

Marissa Cole
Senior Counsel, Privacy & Policy
Redwood Inference

Subject: Re: Initial TIA/questions and subprocessor list request

Hi Sara — thanks for the clear list and the template. Looping in our privacy and legal leads (Marissa and Vivek) so we can get you a consolidated pack. High-level answers up front: 

- We will provide a full subprocessor map with region-level locations and primary datacenter regions (SG, UK, US).
- Evidence pack: SOC 2 Type II report (recent period), encryption summary, and a sample KMS integration doc. We'll indicate which controls apply to dedicated/private deployments vs hosted.
- Contracting approach: we typically execute EU SCCs + UK IDTA/UK Addendum where relevant and can sign a DTIA variant when customers need an explicit TIA document. We'll review your template and propose a short redline.
- Routing/egress: we can route production inference traffic to APAC-only pools for the pilot and restrict cross-region fallbacks unless you authorize them. For support, we can operationally restrict log access and require just-in-time ephemeral access coupled with approvals; we need to confirm the exact wording.

Can we target a consolidated response (map + evidence pack + proposed redline + DocuSign link) by 2026-09-02? Marissa/Vivek — are there any blockers to that timeline?

Regards,
Rafael
Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

Key items we need to provide: 
- Full subprocessor list (include region - e.g., London, Singapore, Oregon) and indicate which subprocessors see raw data vs tokenized/aggregated telemetry.

- Operational routing/egress commitments: propose APAC-only routing for inference + stricter support access controls (JIT + approval + restricted logging). They want to highlight no routine US-based engineer access to production data.

Please advise on the following so I can include in the response: 
1) Which subprocessors we can commit to allow listing in the vendor map (and any we must redact for security reasons).

3) Any known issues with signing a DTIA variant vs insisting on SCCs + TIA.

Thanks — I told Sara we'd aim to bundle everything and send by 9/2. Let me know if you need more time.

-Rafael

2) Routing commitment (pilot)

- SCCs + UK Addendum are our standard; we can also sign a customer DTIA template with a short redline that mirrors SCC/TIA obligations and references the same safeguards. No legal blockers expected for this account.

Evidence pack to prepare: 
- SOC 2 Type II (redacted): available; will note period covered.
- Encryption summary (in transit/at rest) and KMS options (customer-managed KMS for Dedicated/Private).
- Redaction of specific internal IPs in subprocessor map if needed.

Marissa Cole

Redwood Inference

title: Draft MAP: Technical evaluation plan & benchmark targets
preview:
From: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir@healthgrid.com>, Lina Gomez <lina.gomez@healthgrid.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 25 Aug 2026 16:03:00 -0700
Subject: Draft MAP: Technical evaluation plan & benchmark targets

Thanks again for the time today — appreciated the open discussion on HealthGrid's ingestion & NLP needs. Per our discovery, I drafted the technical milestone for the Mutual Action Plan (MAP) so we can align on ownership, success criteria, and a target close. High level below; attached is a spreadsheet with the milestone checklist and initial timelines (20260830_PRIYA_MAP_TechEval_v1.xlsx).

Summary / goal
- Objective: Validate Redwood inference path for HealthGrid ingestion + clinical summarization flows and evaluate cost/latency tradeoffs to inform final SOW and deployment pattern.
- Target close for this MAP milestone: 2026-09-30 (we can tighten if you want an accelerated cadence).

Proposed technical-eval milestones (owners)
1) Test data & access (HealthGrid) — deliver anonymized sample payloads + S3 location. Owner: HealthGrid (Samir, Lina). Target: 2026-08-27.
2) Test harness & model shortlist (Redwood) — build reproducible benchmark harness, draft model shortlist. Owner: Connor O'Brien. Target: 2026-08-28.
3) Benchmarks (Redwood) — run latency/cost and quality experiments against shortlist. Owner: Connor + Rafael. Target: 2026-08-31.
4) Integration POC (HealthGrid + Redwood) — small turnaround integration to validate end-to-end latency and token flow. Owner: HealthGrid engineering + Rafael. Target: 2026-09-10.
5) Readout & go/no-go decision — joint session to review results and confirm recommended deployment (Hosted/Dedicated/Private). Owner: Priya + Marissa. Target: 2026-09-15.

Benchmarks & success criteria (initial ask)
- Latency: p95 <= 180ms, p99 <= 350ms for 512-token peak requests (goal to hit p99 <= 250ms if we prioritize latency).
- Cost: baseline unit cost target <= $0.0025 per 1K tokens for production inference (we'll supply per-model cost estimates in the benchmark runs).
- Quality: ROUGE-L or summary-accuracy thresholds: example tests in the attached sheet; primary acceptance is < 2% critical hallucinations on clinical fields and maintain entity fidelity for patient attributes.
- Model shortlist (starter): Mistral-Instruct-7B, Falcon-40B, Llama2-Chat-70B (quant profiles), plus a Redwood-optimized 16B distill. We'll adjust after we see sample workload characteristics.

Dependencies / blockers
- Need sample payloads with representative sequence-length distribution (long clinical notes + short queries).
- Network peering or a temporary s3 read role for Redwood to access test data (HealthGrid to provide).
- Clarify any data retention/KMS requirements for Private deployment tests (if required later).

Immediate asks for HealthGrid action items
- Please confirm you can share a 1–2k sample set (anonymized) by 2026-08-27 and provide a temporary S3 role or a signed link.
- Send a network diagram if there are any firewall/NAT constraints.

I copied Connor and Rafael to confirm the internal plan; Connor — can you confirm the harness approach & baseline hardware we should target for the runs? Rafael, can you prepare a short estimate of token cost per model variant (ballpark) so Samir has an initial number for procurement?

Thanks — happy to hop on a short follow-up if it's helpful.

Best,
Priya Natarajan
Senior Solutions Manager, Redwood Inference
priya_natarajan@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0132

Attachment(s): 20260830_PRIYA_MAP_TechEval_v1.xlsx


From: Samir Patel <samir@healthgrid.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
Cc: Lina Gomez <lina.gomez@healthgrid.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 27 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft MAP: Technical evaluation plan & benchmark targets

Thanks Priya — good summary and we can meet these timelines. Quick confirmations: we will provision an anonymized sample set (~1.6k documents) and provide a short S3 read-only role by end-of-day 2026-08-27. I attached our network diagram for any peering notes (see healthgrid-network-diagram.pdf).

Priority guidance from our side:
- Latency is the top blocker for user experience — please prioritize p99 and tail latency tuning.
- Quality: hallucination checks around medication/dosage fields are critical. If you can include a short automated check in the harness that flags entity hallucinations, that would be very helpful.
- Cost: procurement wants a per-1M token run-rate estimate (we'll use that for internal budget planning).

We'll plan a 60–90 minute tech session week of 2026-08-31 — propose Tue 2026-09-01 10:00am PT; please confirm.

Samir Patel
CTO, HealthGrid
samir@healthgrid.com

Attachment(s): healthgrid-network-diagram.pdf


From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 28 Aug 2026 11:25:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft MAP: Technical evaluation plan & benchmark targets

+1 on the plan. I'll own the harness and run two tracks: latency/cost (synthetic and replayed traces) and quality (labeled verification set from Samir's samples). Quick notes from an engineering perspective:

Benchmark config (initial)- Hardware: single A100-80GB and a 4-GPU A100 pool for scaled throughput runs (we'll also run a small CPU fallback test).- Sequence lengths: short 64 tokens (query), mid 256 tokens, long 512+ tokens (clinical notes).- Concurrency: single-stream p99/p95 measurement and batched throughput tests with batch sizes [1,4,8].

Model variants to test (run orders)
1) Llama2-Chat-70B (float16)
2) Llama2-Chat-70B (int8/4-bit quant profile A)
3) Falcon-40B (float16)
4) Mistral-Instruct-7B (float16 and quant)
5) Redwood-distill-16B (optimized kernels)

Success thresholds we will report against (to mirror Priya's ask)- p95 latency targets and p99 latency (numbers will be per scenario)- Cost: token-cost estimate per 1M tokens by model/quant/profile- Quality: precision/recall on entity extraction test + manual hallucination spot checks

Immediate internal tasks- I'll create the harness repo and a reproducible script: scripts/run_bench.sh (will include config YAMLs for sequence distribution).- Rafael: please prep a first-pass per-model cost estimate (guesstimate ok) and overlay any Dedicated v Hosted cost delta.

Example harness command (will be in the repo):
# baseline latency run
./scripts/run_bench.sh --model llama2-70b --seq 512 --batch 1 --mode latency --output results/llama2_512_latency.json

I'll post the repo link here once it's up and aim to have initial runs by 2026-08-31 EOD.

Connor O'Brien
Staff Runtime Engineer, Redwood Inference
connor_obrien@redwood.com


From: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir@healthgrid.com>, Lina Gomez <lina.gomez@healthgrid.com>
Cc: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Draft MAP: Technical evaluation plan & benchmark targets — next steps

Thanks Samir — appreciate the fast turn. Confirming next steps and the 2026-09-01 10:00am PT tech session (calendar invite to follow). Brief recap & owners (I put dates in the attached MAP spreadsheet):

Action items
- HealthGrid: deliver anonymized samples + S3 role and network diagram by 2026-08-27 (confirmed).
- Connor: benchmark harness repo and initial runs by 2026-08-31 (in progress).
- Rafael: per-model ballpark cost estimates by 2026-08-30 (please post here).
- Priya & Marissa: schedule readout and prep go/no-go criteria for 2026-09-15.

Attachments included: 20260830_PRIYA_MAP_TechEval_v1.xlsx (milestone checklist, test matrix, acceptance criteria), healthgrid-network-diagram.pdf (from Samir).

Related links: HubSpot deal RDW-4382 and shared Drive folder for technical artifacts: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aBcdExampleTechEval

If anyone needs to expand the model shortlist or tighten the latency targets before we run, please flag today so we can adjust harness configs. Otherwise we'll proceed on the timeline above.

Best,
Priya Natarajan
Senior Solutions Manager, Redwood Inference
priya_natarajan@redwood.com

Attachment(s): 20260830_PRIYA_MAP_TechEval_v1.xlsx, healthgrid-network-diagram.pdf

title: Aurora Systems — throughput snapshot & request to place staging capacity hold (AUR-4723)
preview:
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Quick inventory + soft-hold request for Aurora (AUR-4723)

Team —

Aurora (late-stage, deal AUR-4723) surfaced a confirmed POC sizing today that could convert to an MRC. They want us to guarantee a short exclusive staging window while they complete procurement. Key ask below — can you reply with availability and feasibility within 2 hours?

What Aurora is planning (from discovery):
- Model: Llama2-70B (int8 / Q4-style quantized variant)
- Concurrent workers: 35–45 (they expect burst concurrency for evaluation)
- Target throughput: estimated 2.5–3k tokens/sec sustained during POC peak windows
- Retention: 3–6 months initial reserved capacity; possibility to convert to 12+ mos after commit

Request from me:
1) Quick inventory snapshot (available GPU count by type: A100 80GB, A6000, H100 nodes) that can meet ~3k t/s with acceptable latency (p95 under 250ms).
2) Suggest a minimal configuration we could hard-stake as a soft hold for 2 weeks (i.e., hold without invoicing) and any blockers (network, nvlink ratios, node isolation).
3) If feasible, a shortlist of pricing uplift for reserved staging (monthly rate delta vs on-demand).

Attachments: Aurora_capacity_request_v3.xlsx (attached to external thread later).

I know this is tight — tagging Ben and Chloe because of fleet scheduling and Evelyn on billing/contract impact.

—Rafael
Rafael Mendes
Head of GTM, Redwood Inference
rafael.mendes@redwood.com



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2026-08-29T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Quick inventory + soft-hold request for Aurora (AUR-4723)

Rafael —

Inventory snapshot (current usable capacity as of this morning):
- H100 (1U) pool: 6 nodes immediately free (each node supports ~400–600 t/s depending on quant and batch).
- A100 80GB: 8 GPUs free across 4 nodes (good for model parallel setups, ~250–350 t/s per GPU).
- A6000: 24 GPUs spread across multi-tenant racks (approx 120–180 t/s per GPU for Q8 quantized path).

Rough throughput scenarios (conservative):
- Option A (latency-optimized): 6x H100 nodes -> est 2.4–3k t/s with p95 ~180–260ms.
- Option B (capacity-optimized): 10x A6000 equivalents -> est 2.0–2.8k t/s p95 ~220–300ms.
- Option C (mixed): 4 H100 + 6 A6000 -> best balance for cost & latency (~2.5–3k).

Soft-hold feasibility:
- We can place a soft staging hold (tag resources) for up to 14 days without reallocating billing, provided we can reserve those nodes from the remaining free pool.
- Blocking items: a) if they require dedicated NVLink fabrics (MP interconnect), we'd need A100 nodes with correct cabling — inventory is limited; b) private VPC interconnect setup could add 48–72 hours to readiness.

Pricing delta: approx +18–28% for short-term reserved staging vs spot/on-demand (depends on node mix). If they convert to a 12-month MRC, the delta drops significantly (we can do numbers once Aurora signals).

Actionable: I can place a soft hold on Option C (4 H100 + 6 A6000) for 14 days. Need confirmation from sales that this is the requested staging footprint.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Fleet Capacity Manager, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.patel@aurorasystems.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-08-29T11:20:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Capacity preview for Aurora POC — proposed staging hold (AUR-4723)

Samir —

title: International data residency commitments and DPA annex clarifications — Meridian Biosciences RFI
preview:
From: Claire Evans <claire.evans@meridianbio.com>
To: rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com
Cc: procurement@meridianbio.com, tom.liu@meridianbio.com
Date: 2026-08-17T08:12:00-04:00
Subject: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex Qs

Hi Rafael,

Thanks for taking our RFI. We have a few specific clarifications requested for Section 3 (attached workbook 'Meridian_RFI_Section3.xlsx'). Can you confirm the following at a minimum for the RFP response:

1) Do you offer deterministic routing guarantees for EU-origin requests (i.e., ability to keep processing and model weights within EU regions only)? If so, what contractual commitment can be included in the DPA annex and what operational controls enforce it?

2) If EU routing is requested but capacity in the EU cluster is constrained, what is the documented fallback behavior? Are customers notified and is there an opt-out to block fallback to non-EU processing?

3) Please provide an up-to-date subprocessor matrix indicating which subprocessors may store or process request data and their regions.

4) For data-protection-framework (DPF) evidence: can you provide sample logs/audit artifacts that show regional routing decisions, egress destinations, and a redacted example of a model-serving audit trail?

5) We'd like to see a suggested DPA annex table showing clause mappings for cross-border safeguards and where we can add a Meridian-specific residency paragraph.

Can you get back by end of week? We're consolidating answers for our legal and procurement teams. Happy to set a short call if helpful.

Best,
Claire Evans
Head of IT Procurement
Meridian Biosciences

Attachments: Meridian_RFI_Section3.xlsx (workbook)


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com, jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com, neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com
Date: 2026-08-17T09:05:00-04:00
Subject: Fwd: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex Qs

Team — forwarding Meridian's Section 3 questions. Key asks are deterministic routing, fallback policy, subprocessor list, DPF evidence, and a DPA annex mapping.

Quick notes:
- We already have a routing control we can surface; needs legal language to turn into a DPA paragraph.
- Fallback options: we can describe policy + opt-out capability, but need to confirm notification SLA and whether we can offer hard-block for an enterprise contract.
- Neha, can you generate the subprocessor_matrix.csv with residency columns and last-processed timestamps?
- Kimberly, please draft the DPAAnnex table (start from our template) and include a clause we can offer for Meridian-specific residency commitments.
- Jordan, please prepare the sample audit artifacts we can safely share (redacted).

I'll collate and send to Claire. Target: send initial consolidated answer by EOD 8/17.

Rafael
Director, Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference

[Forwarded message attached: Meridian_RFI_Section3.xlsx]


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>
To: rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com, jordan.blake@jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com, neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com
Date: 2026-08-17T11:20:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex Qs

Rafael — drafting here. Attachments I'm preparing: DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx (text we can include in DPA annex) and subprocessor_matrix.csv (latest). Jordan — can you produce the redacted audit trail by 16:00 ET? Neha — will you confirm whether we can offer a contractual hard-block (no fallback) or only a best-effort notification + opt-out process?

Summary of proposed answers (high-level):
- Deterministic routing: Yes. We offer region-pin routing at the tenant level (customer can require EU-only processing). Operational enforcement: regional routing table + mandatory region-check at edge and within the scheduler; KV cache and model shards are pinned to region.
- Fallback behavior: Default behavior is to queue and scale EU capacity. If capacity exceeds configured thresholds we allow configured fallback to equivalent model variant in alternate region (e.g., eu-west -> us-east). For enterprise contracts we can provide an addendum to either:
  a) enable automatic fallback + notification (default), or
  b) include a residency hard-block that rejects requests when EU capacity isn't sufficient (requires commitment to reserved capacity or longer lead times).
- Subprocessors: CSV will include processor name, role (logging, monitoring, storage), country of operation, and whether they receive encrypted payloads.
- DPF evidence: We can provide redacted routing decision logs, edge geolocation headers, and signed timestamps from the scheduler.

I'll attach drafts in a couple hours.

Kimberly Park
Senior Privacy & Compliance Manager
Redwood Inference

Attachments: DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx, subprocessor_matrix.csv


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Claire Evans <claire.evans@meridianbio.com>
Cc: procurement@meridianbio.com, tom.liu@meridianbio.com
Date: 2026-08-17T15:40:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex clarifications — consolidated response

Hi Claire — thanks for the clear list. Please find our consolidated responses below and the referenced artifacts attached. If anything needs more detail we can follow up with a short call.

1) Deterministic routing capabilities
- Commitment: Redwood can include a residency paragraph in the DPA annex that commits to processing customer requests entirely within the EU region(s) specified by Meridian when region-pin is selected. Proposed contract language is included in 'Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf' (see row 'Residency Commitment - Region Pinning').
- Technical enforcement: requests are accepted at the edge, annotated with customer tenancy and region-pin metadata, and routed through a deterministic routing table in the control plane. Model shards and KV caches for region-pinned workloads are instantiated only in the specified region. We operate an enterprise-only tenancy configuration that prevents cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants.

2) Fallback behavior and opt-out
- Default operational policy: when EU capacity is saturated, the platform will attempt to autoscale EU capacity per the customer's configured autoscale limits. If the request queue exceeds configured thresholds and no EU capacity is available within the configured time window, the system performs one of the following based on the customer's setting:
  a) Allow fallback to an equivalent model variant in a secondary region (with logging and notification), or
  b) Reject the request and return a 409 'region-capacity' response (enterprise-only hard-block).
- Contractual options: we can offer (i) an opt-in fallback with SLA-backed notification and rollback guarantees, or (ii) a hard-block residency guarantee tied to reserved capacity commitments (we recommend reserved capacity for production-critical use-cases).

3) Subprocessor matrix
- Attached: 'subprocessor_matrix.csv' (columns: subprocessor, service, country, processing_purpose, data_access_level, last_audit). We indicate which subprocessors may process or temporarily cache tokenized content and which only handle telemetry/metrics.

4) DPF evidence and audit artifacts
- We are able to provide the following redacted artifacts under NDA:
  - Routing decision log snippet (timestamped, with region tag and decision reason)
  - Scheduler audit with signed timestamp showing model instance and region assignment
  - Edge geolocation headers showing egress IP and ASN evidence
  - Example of a tenant-level access log showing no cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants

Summary of proposed answers (high-level):
- Deterministic routing: Yes. We offer region-pin routing at the tenant level (customer can require EU-only processing). Operational enforcement: regional routing table + mandatory region-check at edge and within the scheduler; KV cache and model shards are pinned to region.
- Fallback behavior: Default behavior is to queue and scale EU capacity. If capacity exceeds configured thresholds we allow configured fallback to equivalent model variant in alternate region (e.g., eu-west -> us-east). For enterprise contracts we can provide an addendum to either:
  a) enable automatic fallback + notification (default), or
  b) include a residency hard-block that rejects requests when EU capacity isn't sufficient (requires commitment to reserved capacity or longer lead times).
- Subprocessors: CSV will include processor name, role (logging, monitoring, storage), country of operation, and whether they receive encrypted payloads.
- DPF evidence: We can provide redacted routing decision logs, edge geolocation headers, and signed timestamps from the scheduler.

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>

Cc: procurement@meridianbio.com, tom.liu@meridianbio.com
Date: 2026-08-17T15:40:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Meridian RFI — Section 3: international transfers and DPA annex clarifications — consolidated response

Hi Claire — thanks for the clear list. Please find our consolidated responses below and the referenced artifacts attached. If anything needs more detail we can follow up with a short call.

1) Deterministic routing capabilities
- Commitment: Redwood can include a residency paragraph in the DPA annex that commits to processing customer requests entirely within the EU region(s) specified by Meridian when region-pin is selected. Proposed contract language is included in 'Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf' (see row 'Residency Commitment - Region Pinning').
- Technical enforcement: requests are accepted at the edge, annotated with customer tenancy and region-pin metadata, and routed through a deterministic routing table in the control plane. Model shards and KV caches for region-pinned workloads are instantiated only in the specified region. We operate an enterprise-only tenancy configuration that prevents cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants.

2) Fallback behavior and opt-out
- Default operational policy: when EU capacity is saturated, the platform will attempt to autoscale EU capacity per the customer's configured autoscale limits. If the request queue exceeds configured thresholds and no EU capacity is available within the configured time window, the system performs one of the following based on the customer's setting:
  a) Allow fallback to an equivalent model variant in a secondary region (with logging and notification), or
  b) Reject the request and return a 409 'region-capacity' response (enterprise-only hard-block).
- Contractual options: we can offer (i) an opt-in fallback with SLA-backed notification and rollback guarantees, or (ii) a hard-block residency guarantee tied to reserved capacity commitments (we recommend reserved capacity for production-critical use-cases).

3) Subprocessor matrix
- Attached: 'subprocessor_matrix.csv' (columns: subprocessor, service, country, processing_purpose, data_access_level, last_audit). We indicate which subprocessors may process or temporarily cache tokenized content and which only handle telemetry/metrics.

4) DPF evidence and audit artifacts
- We are able to provide the following redacted artifacts under NDA:
  - Routing decision log snippet (timestamped, with region tag and decision reason)
  - Scheduler audit with signed timestamp showing model instance and region assignment
  - Edge geolocation headers showing egress IP and ASN evidence
  - Example of a tenant-level access log showing no cross-region replication for region-pinned tenants
Please note: raw logs will be redacted to remove customer PII and model inputs — example artifacts attached (redacted).

5) DPA annex mapping
- Attached: 'Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf' with suggested clause text and mapping to typical Meridian controls (SCCs, technical measures, KMS guidance). We propose a Meridian-specific clause that defines 'Region Pinning' and the fallback options above; language includes the option to require reserved capacity.

Timeline: we can provide the attachments and redacted artifacts by end of day 8/17 (attached). If Meridian requires signed artifacts or additional attestation (e.g., SOC2 report excerpts), allow up to 3 business days for legal review and redaction approvals.

Attachments: Redwood_DPAAnnex_Table.pdf, subprocessor_matrix.csv, DPF_Routing_SLA_draft.docx

title: PrivVPC egress topology and IaC interface review
preview:
From: Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
To: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Jul 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Hi Michael,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We finished drafting an initial VPC topology for our private deployment and have a few open questions before we hand the IaC to our SRE team:

- Egress strategy: we plan to funnel egress through a small set of NAT/bastion subnets (internal-only) but need guidance on how Redwood recommends representing that in Terraform modules so Helm charts and Ansible playbooks can rely on stable egress endpoints.
- Secrets: we currently store certs in HashiVault and rotation hooks are external. Would you prefer customers keep certs in Vault and reference them in Helm via CSI drivers, or should the private deployment package include a KMS-backed secret provider module?
- CI gating: Orchid wants preflight checks to validate that no module creates open 0.0.0.0/0 egress rules. Any example GitLab/Argo gate snippets you can share?

Attaching the rough topology PNG and a short note on our expected downstream consumer services. Can we get a quick review of recommended IaC interface contracts (inputs/outputs and provider expectations) so we can align modules?

Appreciate it — Roberto
Infrastructure Lead
Orchid Systems

Attachment: orchid-privvpc-egress-diagram.png (image/png, 245KB)


From: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Jul 2026 17:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Thanks Roberto — forwarding to infra team to scope an actionable review. High-level notes first:

1) Egress endpoints as a first-class contract
- Recommendation: expose an outputs map from the network Terraform module (e.g., public_egress_ips, nat_gateway_dns) and a small DNS glue module that creates stable private-records in a Route53 (or on-prem DNS) zone. Consumers (Helm/Ansible) should reference the DNS name instead of numeric IPs.
- Keep the Terraform module minimal: provider-agnostic variables (cidr_blocks, az_count, nat_type) + clearly typed outputs. Avoid embedding application-level firewall rules in the network module.

2) Secret handling and rotation
- Prefer customer-owned KMS/Vault for keys. For the package, provide two officially supported secret-provider integrations: HashiCorp Vault CSI driver and a KMS-secrets-provider module (wrapping the cloud KMS or HSM). Provide examples in Helm values referencing the CSI mount path.

3) CI gating and policy checks
- Must include a pre-commit / CI step that runs tfsec and a policy engine (Conftest/OPA) with a Redwoodsafe policy bundle. Example rule: deny security_group.egress where to_cidr_blocks contains 0.0.0.0/0 and description != "egress-bastion-exception".

Action items I will own:
- Draft a short TF module interface spec (inputs/outputs and provider expectations) for Orchid.
- Attach example CI snippets and a minimal Conftest policy.

Irene — can you take the TF interface draft? Grace — can you help produce the Helm values snippet showing CSI mount usage and an Ansible task example for configuring bastion egress? Rafael — once we have artifacts, please slot a security review in next-week sprint.

Thanks,
Michael
Head of Product Ops (Private Deployments)
Redwood Inference


From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Jul 2026 08:21:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Adding concrete notes and a diff of a proposed module surface. I attached a small git diff showing the suggested variable/output names (tf-module-interfaces.diff). Highlights:

- Module variables:
  - name: env_tag (string) — used consistently for resource tagging.
  - name: egress_mode (string enum) — options: ["nat", "proxy", "none"] so modules can gate creation of NAT resources.
  - name: allowed_egress_destinations (list(object)) — structured list for white-listed FQDN/CIDR with reason fields.

- Outputs:
  - egress_dns_map (map(string)) — logical names -> internal DNS names.
  - egress_endpoints (list(string)) — stable hostnames for outbound traffic (used by Helm charts and health checks).

- Conditional resources: use count/for_each keyed off egress_mode to avoid resource drift across providers; add lifecycle prevent_destroy for NAT IPs to prevent accidental release during pruning.

CI snippets attached (ci-gates-snippets.md): examples for GitLab and a GitHub Actions workflow that:
  - runs terraform init/plan (with -out)
  - runs tfsec and Conftest (policy bundle) against plan JSON
  - blocks merge on any deny-level Conftest rule

Security notes: we intentionally do not bake the CSI driver into the Helm umbrella chart; instead we provide a small values.example that shows how to mount the secrets path and a post-deploy job in Ansible that validates mount/permission bits.

I think this addresses Roberto's gating asks — Roberto, if you want, I can convert the interface spec to an rST doc or a small OpenAPI-style schema for automatic validation in CI.

--
Irene Choi
Staff Infrastructure Engineer, Redwood Inference
Attachment: tf-module-interfaces.diff (text/x-patch, 4.1KB)
Attachment: ci-gates-snippets.md (text/markdown, 3.2KB)


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
To: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>, Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Jul 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Thanks all — fast and useful. Short plan:
1) Irene — please publish the tf-module-interfaces.diff as a formal module spec in the private-deploy repo and include a JSON schema for inputs so CI can validate variable shapes. Target: EOD Thu.
2) Grace — add the Helm/CSI example under docs/examples/csi-secret-mount.md and a small Ansible playbook that verifies egress NAT DNS resolution after deploy. Target: Fri sprint demo.
3) Michael — I will open a security review ticket (JIRA-PRIV-4821) and add the Conftest bundle to the infra-policy pipeline.

Roberto — we should be able to run a short preflight on your topology by next week; I'll schedule a 30min sync after Irene publishes the spec.

Acknowledged,
Rafael
Director, Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference
text:
From: Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
To: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 20 Jul 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Hi Michael,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We finished drafting an initial VPC topology for our private deployment and have a few open questions before we hand the IaC to our SRE team:

- Egress strategy: we plan to funnel egress through a small set of NAT/bastion subnets (internal-only) but need guidance on how Redwood recommends representing that in Terraform modules so Helm charts and Ansible playbooks can rely on stable egress endpoints.
- Secrets: we currently store certs in HashiVault and rotation hooks are external. Would you prefer customers keep certs in Vault and reference them in Helm via CSI drivers, or should the private deployment package include a KMS-backed secret provider module?
- CI gating: Orchid wants preflight checks to validate that no module creates open 0.0.0.0/0 egress rules. Any example GitLab/Argo gate snippets you can share?

Attaching the rough topology PNG and a short note on our expected downstream consumer services. Can we get a quick review of recommended IaC interface contracts (inputs/outputs and provider expectations) so we can align modules?

Appreciate it — Roberto
Infrastructure Lead
Orchid Systems

Attachment: orchid-privvpc-egress-diagram.png (image/png, 245KB)


From: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>
To: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Jul 2026 17:05:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Thanks Roberto — forwarding to infra team to scope an actionable review. High-level notes first:

1) Egress endpoints as a first-class contract
- Recommendation: expose an outputs map from the network Terraform module (e.g., public_egress_ips, nat_gateway_dns) and a small DNS glue module that creates stable private-records in a Route53 (or on-prem DNS) zone. Consumers (Helm/Ansible) should reference the DNS name instead of numeric IPs.
- Keep the Terraform module minimal: provider-agnostic variables (cidr_blocks, az_count, nat_type) + clearly typed outputs. Avoid embedding application-level firewall rules in the network module.

2) Secret handling and rotation
- Prefer customer-owned KMS/Vault for keys. For the package, provide two officially supported secret-provider integrations: HashiCorp Vault CSI driver and a KMS-secrets-provider module (wrapping the cloud KMS or HSM). Provide examples in Helm values referencing the CSI mount path.

3) CI gating and policy checks
- Must include a pre-commit / CI step that runs tfsec and a policy engine (Conftest/OPA) with a Redwoodsafe policy bundle. Example rule: deny security_group.egress where to_cidr_blocks contains 0.0.0.0/0 and description != "egress-bastion-exception".

Action items I will own:
- Draft a short TF module interface spec (inputs/outputs and provider expectations) for Orchid.
- Attach example CI snippets and a minimal Conftest policy.

Irene — can you take the TF interface draft? Grace — can you help produce the Helm values snippet showing CSI mount usage and an Ansible task example for configuring bastion egress? Rafael — once we have artifacts, please slot a security review in next-week sprint.

Thanks,
Michael
Head of Product Ops (Private Deployments)
Redwood Inference


From: Irene Choi <irene.choi@redwood.ai>
To: Michael Grant <michael.grant@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
Cc: Roberto Diaz <roberto.diaz@orchid.com>
Date: Tue, 21 Jul 2026 08:21:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Private VPC egress topology + IaC interface questions

Adding concrete notes and a diff of a proposed module surface. I attached a small git diff showing the suggested variable/output names (tf-module-interfaces.diff). Highlights:

- Module variables:
  - name: env_tag (string) — used consistently for resource tagging.
  - name: egress_mode (string enum) — options: ["nat", "proxy", "none"] so modules can gate creation of NAT resources.
  - name: allowed_egress_destinations (list(object)) — structured list for white-listed FQDN/CIDR with reason fields.

- Outputs:
  - egress_dns_map (map(string)) — logical names -> internal DNS names.
  - egress_endpoints (list(string)) — stable hostnames for outbound traffic (used by Helm charts and health checks).

- Conditional resources: use count/for_each keyed off egress_mode to avoid resource drift across providers; add lifecycle prevent_destroy for NAT IPs to prevent accidental release during pruning.

CI snippets attached (ci-gates-snippets.md): examples for GitLab and a GitHub Actions workflow that:
  - runs terraform init/plan (with -out)
  - runs tfsec and Conftest (policy bundle) against plan JSON
  - blocks merge on any deny-level Conftest rule

Security notes: we intentionally do not bake the CSI driver into the Helm umbrella chart; instead we provide a small values.example that shows how to mount the secrets path and a post-deploy job in Ansible that validates mount/permission bits.

I think this addresses Roberto's gating asks — Roberto, if you want, I can convert the interface spec to an rST doc or a small OpenAPI-style schema for automatic validation in CI.

--
Irene Choi
Staff Infrastructure Engineer, Redwood Inference
Attachment: tf-module-interfaces.diff (text/x-patch, 4.1KB)
Attachment: ci-gates-snippets.md (text/markdown, 3.2KB)

2) Grace — add the Helm/CSI example under docs/examples/csi-secret-mount.md and a small Ansible playbook that verifies egress NAT DNS resolution after deploy. Target: Fri sprint demo.
3) Michael — I will open a security review ticket (JIRA-PRIV-4821) and add the Conftest bundle to the infra-policy pipeline.

From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.ai>

Subject: KiteMed trial: access, success criteria & credentialing plan

Hi Alex —

Great to meet earlier. Quick recap from our side and proposed next steps for the 3‑week trial so we can get your engineering and security teams unblocked:

Scope we agreed on:
- Basic API integration to validate throughput and latency on a representative patient-messaging workload (approx 20–50k messages / 3 weeks).
- A short quality evaluation on 200 sample prompts across triage, appointment-scheduling, and messaging templates.

Proposed success criteria (please confirm/edit):
1) 95th percentile response latency <= 250ms for typical 64‑token completion workload
2) No SLA-affecting errors during 1k query window (target uptime 99%)
3) Qualitative accuracy: >= 85% pass rate on the 200 prompt eval set by KiteMed SMEs

Access & credentialing we need from KiteMed:
- API key creation: please indicate preferred scope. We recommend a trial key with read/write eval scope and a 3‑week expiry.
- IP allowlist: we'll provide our public egress ranges to whitelist (we also support SSO).
- SSO invite: if you want to gate the Redwood Console access, please add redwood-trial@kitemed.com to your Okta org (SAML).
- Sample data: a representative CSV (PII redacted) and the 200 prompt set for quality checks.

Owners & timelines (tentative):
- KiteMed SRE / Security to enable allowlist and SSO by Tue 21 Jul
- Redwood (Ben + Rafael) to provision trial keys and environment by Wed 22 Jul
- Kickoff run + eval: Thu 23 Jul — Fri 09 Aug (3 weeks)

Next steps: can you confirm SRE contact and procurement point of contact? Once we have that we’ll share the checklist and create the HubSpot plan.

title: Nimbus evaluation: hyperscaler interop & latency/throughput test plan
preview:
From: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
To: Taylor Simmons <taylor@nimbusanalytics.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-14T15:04:00-07:00
Subject: Recap + next steps from today's call — baseline tests and hyperscaler comparison

Taylor — thanks again for taking time today. Short recap and proposed next steps so we can make our side of the proof-of-concept frictionless.

1) What we heard from you
- Primary goal: determine if Redwood can meet Nimbus' p95/p99 latency SLOs for interactive product searches (target p95 < 150ms for 128-token requests).
- Secondary: cost per 1k tokens and throughput targets for peak traffic (aiming for > 1.2M tokens/hour during batch rebuild windows).
- Constraint: you need runs that compare Redwood hosted endpoints vs two hyperscaler-managed endpoints you already use for inference.

2) Proposed experiment (high level)
- A/B runs across three endpoints: Redwood hosted (small-medium instance), Hyperscaler-A, Hyperscaler-B.
- Workload: 128-token and 512-token distributions (70/30 split), warm cache and cold-cache runs.
- Metrics: p50, p95, p99 latencies; tokens/sec; sustained throughput at 95th percentile; failure rate; cost estimate (compute / token).
- Artifacts: run logs, CSV of raw latencies, aggregate plots.

3) Immediate asks / owners
- Rafael: provision the benchmark harness and a Redwood-only baseline (GPU config, model quantization used) — target initial run by EOD Fri.
- Marissa: confirm pricing guardrails we'll present to Nimbus and whether we can extend a one-month trial credit for this benchmark.
- Taylor: send the sample request set (JSONL) and the hyperscaler endpoints/credentials you want us to call.

Attachments: benchmark_instructions.md (test steps + required headers), and a short runbook. I'll drop the GH link in a follow-up.

Cheers,
Jordan
Jordan Blake
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.com>
Cc: Taylor Simmons <taylor@nimbusanalytics.com>
Date: 2026-07-14T17:28:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Recap + next steps from today's call — baseline tests and hyperscaler comparison

Jordan — got it. Quick confirmation of the test plan and what I'll run:

Planned test matrix (initial run)
- Models: rwinference-7b-quant (Redwood), HS-A-gpt-7b (Hyperscaler-A), HS-B-gpt-7b (Hyperscaler-B)
- Sequences: 128 (70%), 512 (30%)
- Concurrency modes: 1, 4, 16 concurrent clients to capture latency tail behavior
- Warm vs cold: 10-minute warm-up then 30-minute steady-state window, plus cold-cache single-shot runs
- Batch settings: continuous batching on Redwood; disabled on HS endpoints if not exposed — I'll mimic their default behavior where possible.

Metrics capture
- p50/p95/p99 latency (ms), tokens/sec, request/sec, 95th percentile throughput, error rates, and GPU utilization (where metrics available).
- I'll produce raw CSV (latency_v1_results.csv) and a summarized dashboard snapshot.

Notes / blockers
- Need the sample request set from Taylor and the hyperscaler endpoint URLs + any auth tokens. If HS endpoints do not permit synthetic load from our IPs, we may need Nimbus to run their side of the harness (we can provide the script).
- For apples-to-apples, I propose we lock model configs (quantization level, sequence padding) to match as closely as possible.

I'll aim to post the first-run CSV by 2026-07-17T02:00:00Z. Rafael

Taylor — thanks again for taking time today. Short recap and proposed next steps so we can make our side of the proof-of-concept frictionless.

1) What we heard from you
- Primary goal: determine if Redwood can meet Nimbus' p95/p99 latency SLOs for interactive product searches (target p95 < 150ms for 128-token requests).
- Secondary: cost per 1k tokens and throughput targets for peak traffic (aiming for > 1.2M tokens/hour during batch rebuild windows).
- Constraint: you need runs that compare Redwood hosted endpoints vs two hyperscaler-managed endpoints you already use for inference.

2) Proposed experiment (high level)
- A/B runs across three endpoints: Redwood hosted (small-medium instance), Hyperscaler-A, Hyperscaler-B.
- Workload: 128-token and 512-token distributions (70/30 split), warm cache and cold-cache runs.
- Metrics: p50, p95, p99 latencies; tokens/sec; sustained throughput at 95th percentile; failure rate; cost estimate (compute / token).
- Artifacts: run logs, CSV of raw latencies, aggregate plots.

3) Immediate asks / owners
- Rafael: provision the benchmark harness and a Redwood-only baseline (GPU config, model quantization used) — target initial run by EOD Fri.
- Marissa: confirm pricing guardrails we'll present to Nimbus and whether we can extend a one-month trial credit for this benchmark.

Attachments: benchmark_instructions.md (test steps + required headers), and a short runbook. I'll drop the GH link in a follow-up.

Cheers,
Jordan

title: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency
preview:
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cole <sam.cole@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-12T02:10:00-07:00
Subject: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency

Hi Sam —

We observed a sharp uptick in tail latency for short-sequence generation (p95/p99) affecting several Acme endpoints starting ~2026-07-12T01:40Z. Root cause appears to be a scheduler regression in the runtime that increases small-batch scheduling contention under certain traffic patterns.

We’re preparing a hotfix that addresses a race in the scheduler's ready queue which should restore normal latency characteristics. We plan a short, rolling maintenance window starting 2026-07-12T03:30Z UTC; each pool will be patched and health-checked (expected per-pool downtime ~30–60s).

What we’re doing now:
- Isolating impacted pools and diverting new requests to warm fallbacks.
- Building and validating a hotfix binary (smoke + perf tests).
- Preparing rollback artifacts and monitoring runbooks.

Immediate asks:
1) Please confirm you’ve seen increased latency on your side and whether any requests were retried/failing.
2) If a maintenance window at 03:30Z works, we’ll proceed and post a status update to the incident page.

Attachments: hotfix-package-linux.tar.gz (patch build + smoke test summary).

We’ll keep you updated in this thread; sorry for the disruption and thanks for the quick confirmation.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Head of Sales Engineering, Redwood Inference
(630) 555-0182

---


From: Sam Cole <sam.cole@acme-corp.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
Cc: acme-platform-ops@acme-corp.com
Date: 2026-07-12T02:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency

Thanks Karthik — we did see elevated p95/p99 around 01:45–02:00Z and a handful of client retries surfaced as errors in our logs (roughly 0.2% of calls). No customer-facing incidents yet, but we’ve got alerts firing.

03:30Z is OK on our side. Two quick Qs: 1) will the patch affect persisted KV cache behavior? 2) Any guidance for client-side retry/backoff during the rolling window?

Sam
Sam Cole
Platform Reliability Engineer, Acme Corp

On Tue, Jul 12, 2026 at 02:10, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai> wrote:
> We observed a sharp uptick in tail latency...


From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cole <sam.cole@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-12T02:35:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency

Hi Sam — quick answers:
- The fix is at the scheduler layer and does not modify model weights or persisted KV cache layout. We expect cache semantics to be preserved.
- During the ~30–60s per-pool rolling restarts we recommend exponential backoff with jitter; avoid aggressive retries. We’ll mark affected zones as degraded in the status link below.

We will also disable request multiplexing briefly while the rollouts complete to minimize warm-start contention. I’ll be on the call during rollout and will post minute-by-minute updates.

Status: https://status.redwood.ai/incidents/2026-07-12-42

Laura Bennett
Director, Product Operations

> On Tue, Jul 12, 2026 at 02:22, Sam Cole <sam.cole@acme-corp.com> wrote:
> > Thanks Karthik —...


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cole <sam.cole@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-12T03:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency

Update before rollout:
- Hotfix has passed smoke + perf validation on a staging fleet (p50/p95 within baseline).
- Rollout plan: apply to non-tenant pools first, then Acme pools; per-pool canary (5% of capacity) for 10m, then full pool patch.
- P0 on-call: Evelyn Hart (evelyn@redwood.ai) — escalation to Rafael if rollback needed.

We expect the maintenance to start 03:30Z and to finish for Acme within ~20 minutes total given their current pool assignments. I’ll post a note when we begin and again when the Acme pools are green.

—Karthik

> On Tue, Jul 12, 2026 at 02:35, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > Hi Sam — quick answers...

text:
From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cole <sam.cole@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.ai>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-07-12T02:10:00-07:00
Subject: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency

Hi Sam —

We observed a sharp uptick in tail latency for short-sequence generation (p95/p99) affecting several Acme endpoints starting ~2026-07-12T01:40Z. Root cause appears to be a scheduler regression in the runtime that increases small-batch scheduling contention under certain traffic patterns.

We’re preparing a hotfix that addresses a race in the scheduler's ready queue which should restore normal latency characteristics. We plan a short, rolling maintenance window starting 2026-07-12T03:30Z UTC; each pool will be patched and health-checked (expected per-pool downtime ~30–60s).

What we’re doing now:
- Isolating impacted pools and diverting new requests to warm fallbacks.
- Building and validating a hotfix binary (smoke + perf tests).
- Preparing rollback artifacts and monitoring runbooks.

Immediate asks:
1) Please confirm you’ve seen increased latency on your side and whether any requests were retried/failing.
2) If a maintenance window at 03:30Z works, we’ll proceed and post a status update to the incident page.

Attachments: hotfix-package-linux.tar.gz (patch build + smoke test summary).

We’ll keep you updated in this thread; sorry for the disruption and thanks for the quick confirmation.

—Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Head of Sales Engineering, Redwood Inference
(630) 555-0182

---

Date: 2026-07-12T02:35:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Immediate maintenance: urgent scheduler patch affecting inference latency

Hi Sam — quick answers:
- The fix is at the scheduler layer and does not modify model weights or persisted KV cache layout. We expect cache semantics to be preserved.
- During the ~30–60s per-pool rolling restarts we recommend exponential backoff with jitter; avoid aggressive retries. We’ll mark affected zones as degraded in the status link below.

We will also disable request multiplexing briefly while the rollouts complete to minimize warm-start contention. I’ll be on the call during rollout and will post minute-by-minute updates.

Status: https://status.redwood.ai/incidents/2026-07-12-42

Laura Bennett
Director, Product Operations

Subject: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Mina / Redwood team,

We flipped the private connector for our on-prem cluster to the new VPC peer this morning (routed via the edge box). Since then we've seen intermittent spikes in latency and TCP retransmits to Redwood endpoints (api.redwood.example). The problem appears to be asymmetric: some flows go fine but a subset sees repeated retransmits and then recovers.

Attached is a small pcap capture (tcpdump -w) and a traceroute from one of our application nodes. Kernel logs around the time of the issue are in dmesg-snippet-07jul.txt. We haven't changed app config — only routed traffic via the new connector and applied the SG changes per the cutover doc.

Can the Redwood networking on-call take a look? This is affecting transaction latency for a few of our merchants.

Thanks,
Sanjay
Platform Ops — Helix Payments

---

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
Cc: Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>, networking@helixpay.com
Date: Wed, 7 Jul 2026 15:28:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Sanjay — thanks for the quick heads up and the attachments. Putting the networking team on this thread.

Quick ask to help triage faster:
- Can you confirm whether the flows showing retransmits are NAT'd (source IP translated) at your edge or are they preserving origin IPs across the connector?
- If possible, run: sudo iptables -t nat -L -n -v and paste the NAT table output (or export).
- If you can, run a 5s ping flood and start a tcpdump capturing just one 5-tuple for a failing connection (we can guide filter).

We will look at the pcap and traceroute immediately.

— Mina
Staff Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

---

From: Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>
To: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
Cc: Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Jul 2026 15:47:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Sanjay — I pulled the pcap. Observations:
- Traceroute shows one hop with a 50ms blip then next hop drops packets intermittently (hop 5 in their path).
- PCAP shows client sends SYN, server SYN-ACK arrives but we also see duplicated SYN-ACKs and subsequent ACK retransmits. Sequence suggests packet reordering or middlebox memcpy.
- dmesg snippet has repeated kernel messages: "NETDEV WATCHDOG: eth1 (r8152): transmit timeout" around 14:34:12 — can you confirm NIC drivers/firmware on the node?

Sanjay: can you paste output of `ip route get <redwood-eu-ip>` for one of the nodes that saw retransmits? Also we need your NAT table (mina asked for this).

Tessa
Sr. NetOps | Redwood Inference

---

From: Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
To: Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>
Cc: networking@helixpay.com
Date: Wed, 7 Jul 2026 16:09:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Thanks — attaching the NAT table and a couple more tcpdumps. For the nodes in question the edge device does SNAT to the connector IP (so NAT is in play).

iptables -t nat -L -n -v (snippet):
Chain PREROUTING (policy ACCEPT 0 packets, 0 bytes)
Chain POSTROUTING (policy ACCEPT 1203 packets, 72K bytes)
  MASQUERADE  all  --  10.0.0.0/16        anywhere

We are on kernel 5.10.23 with vendor r8152 driver. We'll upgrade if needed but wanted to triage network path first.

Attachments: trace-20260707-helix.pcap, sg-export-helix-07jul.json

Sanjay

---

From: Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>
To: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Jul 2026 17:12:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Quick hypothesis from the packet pattern and the kernel logs: MTU/MSS clamping or an on-path firewall doing segmentation. The duplicated SYN-ACKs + retransmits and a transmit timeout in dmesg point to upstream interface drops and retransmit cascades. Two immediate checks:
1) Confirm MTU on the application host, edge device, and the connector interface. Run `ip link show` and `ip -s link`.
2) Check for iptables conntrack and NAT timeouts; large numbers of conntrack entries and an aggressive conntrack timeout can cause mid-connection drops if translations are reaped.

I'd also like to see the Security Group export (you attached) — specifically any stateful deny rules that might be hitting related connections on return path.

Rafael — wondering if you recall a recent kernel bug with r8152 and fragmented jumbo frames?

— Hiro

---

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
To: Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Jul 2026 18:03:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

I dug into the pcap. Two additional notes:
- There are ICMP "Fragmentation needed" messages in the capture coming back from the connector path. That strongly suggests MTU mismatch on the on-prem edge or the path where PMTU can't complete because of blocked ICMPs.
- The connection pattern shows SNAT on egress, but replies are arriving to original IPs (asymmetric path) and being dropped by the edge because there is no conntrack entry for those reverse-flow packets. That can happen when the forward path gets SNAT'd but the return path traverses a different egress without the same SNAT.

Action items I recommend immediately:
- Apply MSS clamp on the edge device (set TCPMSS to 1400) so hosts don't send large segments that require fragmentation.
- Ensure ICMP type 3 code 4 (fragmentation needed) is allowed between the two edges so PMTU works.
- If you run MASQUERADE on multiple egress points, coordinate and prefer a single NAT ingress/egress or use SNAT with consistent mapping.

I can provide iptables snippets:
`iptables -t mangle -A FORWARD -p tcp --tcp-flags SYN,RST SYN -j TCPMSS --clamp-mss-to-pmtu`
Or explicitly set: `--set-mss 1400` if clamp is unreliable.

I'll standby to help patch rules.

— Rafael

---

From: Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>
To: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>, Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Jul 2026 07:05:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Scheduling note: I'd like a 30-minute war room at 09:00 EDT to apply changes and observe — Mina, Rafael, can you join? Sanjay — can your networking team be available to apply edge config and roll back if needed?

Also, please capture `ip -s link`, `cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward`, and `sysctl -a | grep conntrack` before making changes. We'll record the change window for postmortem.

Thanks,
Grace
Head of Customer Reliability | Redwood Inference

---

From: Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>
To: Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Jul 2026 09:02:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Update from war room: our team applied MSS clamp to 1400 on the edge (iptables mangle rule) and opened ICMP type 3 code 4 between the two edges. We also consolidated SNAT to a single egress address for flows to Redwood for the test window.

Immediate results: retransmits dropped to near-zero and p95 latency for the affected flows improved from ~220ms to ~28ms. We've been monitoring for ~2 hours with no recurrence.

Attachments: dmesg-snippet-07jul.txt (post-change), trace-20260707-helix.pcap (annotated)

Sanjay

---

From: Mina Haddad <mina_haddad@redwood.com>
To: Sanjay Agarwal <sanjay@helixpay.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Tessa Morgan <tessa_morgan@redwood.com>, Hiro Tanaka <hiro_tanaka@redwood.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Jul 2026 11:02:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Intermittent TCP retransmits since private connector flip

Excellent work, team. Thank you for the quick coordination and for keeping rollback ready.

Next steps:
- Sanjay, please keep the MSS clamp and SNAT consolidation during the 24h monitoring window and we will reconvene after 24h to confirm closure.
- Rafael/Tessa/Hiro: we'll add a small playbook entry to the private connector troubleshooting guide covering MTU/ICMP checks and NAT asymmetry scenarios. I'll take the action to author a short runbook and upload to the customer folder.
- Grace: please open a postmortem doc and mark owner for follow-up items.

I'll mark this resolved for now and update the ticket once the 24h window passes.

— Mina
Customer Reliability | Redwood Inference

title: Follow-up: Spectra AI trial - evaluation charter & KPIs
preview:
From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Bates <emma.bates@spectra-ai.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: Spectra AI trial - evaluation charter & KPIs

Hi Emma —

Thanks again for the demo earlier this morning. Per request, here’s a concise evaluation charter we discussed to make the Spectra trial actionable for both sides. I put estimated owners and a proposed 4-week timeline below. Please confirm or flag changes.

High-level POC goal:
- Validate Redwood Inference can meet Spectra’s production latency/throughput and maintain acceptable cost per inference while preserving generation quality on Product Summarization pipelines.

Success criteria (pass/fail checkpoints):
1) Latency: P95 <= 120ms for 512-token summaries at 8 concurrent users (target 100ms).
2) Throughput: sustained 120 requests/sec across two endpoints (summarize + rerank) for peak window.
3) Cost: inference cost <= $0.00035 per token (end-to-end billable cost estimate).
4) Quality: automated metric + human review:
   - Automated: ROUGE-L delta within 2 points of baseline model on our shared test-set.
   - Human: >= 88% accept rate from Spectra annotators on 200 sampled outputs (binary accept/revise).
5) Reliability: <= 1% error rate, 99.5% availability during test window.

Evaluation plan / timeline (proposed):
- Week 0 (this week): kickoff, access & sample data transfer, config choices (model size, token limits, quant/profile). Owner: Jasmine.
- Week 1: Integration and smoke tests (connect staging, ingest sample corpus). Owner: Spectra Eng (Emma) + Jasmine.
- Week 2: Run controlled load tests + cost profiling. Owner: Karthik.
- Week 3: Human evaluation + tuning (prompt templates, fallback policies). Owner: Spectra PM + Rafael.
- Week 4: Final report, recommendations, and handoff. Owner: Jasmine + Rafael.

Immediate asks for Spectra:
- Shared test-set (anonymized) and 200 human evaluators for week-3 review.
- Temp staging credentials for eng@spectra-ai.com.

Next steps (for this week):
- Rafael to send SOW/quote for the 4-week trial and confirm procurement path (ETA 48 hours).
- Spectra to share sample dataset and list of evaluation annotators by EOD Thu (18 June).
- Jasmine to provision staging environment and share endpoint details.

Attachments: trial checklist + preliminary quote (see attached).

Thanks — I’ll follow up with slots for a 30-min kickoff tomorrow.

Best,

Jasmine Liu
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
jasmine.liu@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192

Redwood Inference — Production-grade LLM inference


From: Emma Bates <emma.bates@spectra-ai.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.ai>
Cc: Spectra AI Engineering <eng@spectra-ai.com>
Date: Mon, 15 Jun 2026 11:07:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Follow-up: Spectra AI trial - evaluation charter & KPIs

Thanks Jasmine — this is great and exactly the level of detail we needed. A few quick notes/questions: 

- Latency: 120ms p95 is acceptable as an upper bound, but our product SLA target is 95ms p95 in prod; if we can consistently hit 100ms in trial that’d be excellent. Can we add a secondary KPI showing p50/p90 as well for context?
- Cost: $0.00035/token looks straightforward, but we need an end-to-end example for a typical 400-token request (tokens in/out + pre/post processing) — can Karthik include that in the cost profile?
- Quality: Spectra will provide the 200 annotators; we propose a 5-point grading scale and will convert to an accept rate threshold (>=88% as you suggested).

We’ll confirm dataset delivery by Thu EOD. Kickoff tomorrow 10:00am ET works for our engineering lead.

Also, for procurement: we’ll need a simple test agreement (NDA + trial terms) — Rafael, can you flag what level of redlines are typical?

Appreciate the clear plan — Emma

Emma Bates
Senior Product Manager, Spectra AI
emma.bates@spectra-ai.com


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Bates <emma.bates@spectra-ai.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 16 Jun 2026 08:35:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Follow-up: Spectra AI trial - evaluation charter & KPIs

Emma / Jasmine — quick follow-up with the cost & throughput profiling plan. Karthik here (infra lead) — I’ll own the load tests and cost breakdown. Actions I’m taking: 

- Will run token-level cost simulations for the 400-token example (encoding + model + postprocessing) and deliver a line-item estimate by Thu 18 Jun EOD.
- I’ll add P50/P90/P95 latency targets to the report and include a latency distribution chart for the final deliverable.
- For throughput, my baseline is a 2-GPU dedicated pool for the trial; I’ll test burst-to-200req/s and sustained 120req/s with KV cache enabled.

One note on instrumentation: please make sure the staging env will allow us to emit traces and per-request token counts (we’ll share a minimal telemetry spec).

I’ve attached the trial checklist and the preliminary RA quote Rafael prepared.

Thanks,
Karthik
Principal Infrastructure Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.ai>
To: Emma Bates <emma.bates@spectra-ai.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 18 Jun 2026 14:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Follow-up: Spectra AI trial - evaluation charter & KPIs

Emma — quick procurement note: I’ve circulated the trial SOW and a short-term trial agreement to your procurement alias (procure@spectra-ai.com). Key commercial points in the SOW: trial run-rate, billing uplift for dedicated GPU hours outside the baseline, and data handling/retention (30 days).

If they push back on the data retention window we can toggle to customer-managed KMS for the trial — that typically satisfies enterprise security teams.

Attached: redwood-poc-quote-RA.pdf (line-item pricing) and spectra-trial-checklist.pdf.

Next steps:
- Spectra to confirm dataset & procurement contacts by EOD today (18 Jun).
- Jasmine to schedule 10:00am ET kickoff tomorrow (19 Jun).
- Karthik to send telemetry spec and cost example by EOD.

Rafael Mendes
Head of Commercial Ops, Redwood Inference
